attributes.stem:
A 25-year-old woman presents to the emergency department feeling fatigued and light-headed. These symptoms have been worsening over the last five days, when she has experienced heavy recent menstrual bleeding.

A blood test shows a haemoglobin of 65 g/L. She is immediately transfused with a single unit of packed red blood cells, given over two hours. However, 20 minutes after the start of the transfusion, she complains of flank pain and shortness of breath. Vital signs show the following:

  • Temperature 38.3 ℃

  • Blood pressure 88/60 mmHg

  • Heart rate 110/min

  • Respiratory rate 16/min

  • Oxygen saturation 98% on room air

A urinary catheter is inserted. Her urine bag from her catheter shows 30mL of red-brown urine. Cardiorespiratory examination and the bedside ECG showed no abnormal results.
attributes.stemQuestion: Apart from terminating the transfusion, which one of the following is the next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has developed fever, chills, shortness of breath, hypotension and flank pain occurring rapidly after the onset of transfusion. This suggestive of an acute haemolytic transfusion reaction, most commonly due to implicated red blood cell (RBC) antigens of the ABO blood group due to clerical error or the lack of cross-matching. Acute haemolytic transfusion reactions occur within minutes of the transfusion, however, symptoms may not be appreciated. At times, the change of urine colour to red-brown may be the only sign associated with this transfusion reaction. Immediate management is required which involves stopping the transfusion immediately in addition to immediate normal saline infusion to the reduce the risk of renal failure and hypotension. Repeat typing and cross-matching of the transfusion product must be done to identify and record causative blood products.

Transfusion-related acute lung injury presents with sudden onset of hypoxaemic respiratory insufficiency shortly after transfusion. Transfusion related acute lung injury may also present with shortness of breath and fever, but it is usually associated with pulmonary oedema or abnormal lung sounds, and the chest X-ray would show diffused infiltrates. Management may involve maintaining ventilation and oxygen supplementation and anti-inflammatory therapy with IV steroids may be necessary as well. 

Type 1 hypersensitivity reaction, or anaphylaxis, may occur during blood product transfusion. It presents as bronchospasms, hypotensive shock and possible urticaria. Management involves immediate adrenaline administration.

Transfusion-associated circulatory overload is another transfusion reaction, due to volume overload secondary to transfusion of blood products. It presents within the first six hours of transfusion and is characterised by hypertension, wide pulse pressure and symptoms of volume overload. Management involves fluid mobilisation via diuretics such as furosemide.

Non-haemolytic febrile transfusion reactions may also occur, however, this usually presents as fever, malaise and chills approximately 1 – 6 hours after transfusion. Cessation of transfusion products and treatment with acetaminophen is the mainstay in management.
learningPoints: Acute haemolytic transfusion reaction should be treated with generous fluid resuscitation and termination of the transfusion.
references: Murphy MF, Casbard AC, Ballard S, et al. Prevention of bedside errors in transfusion medicine (PROBE-TM) study: a cluster-randomized, matched-paired clinical areas trial of a simple intervention to reduce errors in the pretransfusion bedside check. Transfusion 2007; 47:771.
Carson JL, Grossman BJ, Kleinman S, et al. Red blood cell transfusion: a clinical practice guideline from the AABB*. Ann Intern Med 2012; 157:49.
